16: A revelation of God: 'My beloved is mine, and I am his.' The Song's vision of mutual possession between lover and beloved is the OT's most intimate description of the covenant relationship. In the NT, this mutual possession is fulfilled in the Christ-church relationship: 'You are not your own, for you were bought with a price' (1 Corinthians 6:19-20); 'I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine' becomes 'I live, and yet not I, but Christ lives in me' (Galatians 2:20). The mutual knowing of bride and groom (I know my sheep and my sheep know me, John 10:14) is the new covenant's personal form of the Song's mutual possession. The eschatological fulfillment is the marriage of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7-9; Song of Solomon 21:2): the Song's vision of complete love is consummated in the new creation when the Bride has made herself ready.
1: A shadow: 'I am a wildflower of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.' The bride's self-description is modest — a common wildflower, not a cultivated ornament. The NT's Christological reading sees here the humility of Christ who 'made himself nothing, taking the form of a servant' (Philippians 2:7). He is not the rose in the formal garden but the flower of the field — accessible to anyone, everywhere. The church that follows him shares this same lowly beauty in the world's eyes.
2: A revelation of God: 'Like a lily among thorns, so is my beloved among the young women.' The beloved is beautiful precisely in contrast to what surrounds her — thorns cannot diminish the lily's beauty but only throw it into relief. The church's beauty in the world follows the same logic: surrounded by the darkness of sin, the church's distinctiveness is her very loveliness. Christ sees his people as beautiful even in a world that cannot see them that way.
3: A shadow: The beloved is like an apple tree among the trees of the forest — providing shade and fruit, not merely beauty. 'With great delight I sat in his shade, and his fruit was sweet to my taste.' John 6:35 (I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger) and John 15:5 (I am the vine; whoever abides in me bears much fruit) develop the same nourishment-and-shade imagery. Christ is shade from the heat of judgment (Isaiah 25:4) and fruit that satisfies the deepest hunger.
4: A shadow: 'He brought me to the banquet hall, and his banner over me was love.' The banquet hall (beit hayayin, house of wine) is the place of celebration and feasting — the OT image of eschatological blessing (Isaiah 25:6; Song of Solomon 55:1-2). The banner of love — a military standard planted over conquered territory — declares the territory of the beloved as belonging to the lover. Paul's exposition of Christ's love (Ephesians 3:17-19: the love that surpasses knowledge) is the banner that covers the church. The eschatological fulfillment is the wedding feast of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9).
5: A revelation of God: 'Strengthen me with raisins; revive me with apples — for I am faint with love.' The faintness is not distress but the overwhelming excess of love — the soul undone by the intensity of the Beloved's love. Paul's prayer in Ephesians 3:14-19 uses the same structure: he prays that believers 'may have strength to comprehend ... the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge' — knowing that its full comprehension is beyond human capacity. The soul strengthened by the fruits of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22) is the NT's form of 'strengthen me with raisins.'
6: A revelation of God: 'His left arm is under my head, and his right arm holds me close.' The complete embrace: the lover supports and holds simultaneously. The image of divine arms beneath and around is the OT's expression of omnipresent care (Deuteronomy 33:27: 'the eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms'). Christ's care for the church is not from a distance but an embrace — the Good Shepherd lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11) and carries them (Luke 15:5).
7: A revelation of God: The adjuration not to awaken love prematurely — 'do not rouse or awaken love until it pleases' — is the divine sovereignty over timing in the covenant relationship. The kairos of grace cannot be forced; intimacy with God comes on his schedule. The NT parallel is 'do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed' (Ephesians 4:30) — the Spirit of love is not to be coerced or manipulated. The covenant relationship operates on divine initiative and divine timing throughout.
8: A type: 'Listen — it is my beloved! Here he comes, leaping across the mountains, bounding over the hills.' The approach of the beloved from a distance, coming quickly and eagerly — this is the structure of the incarnation and the parousia. The Word who was with God 'leapt' into the world at the incarnation (Wis 18:15 uses this language for the divine Word entering Egypt; the image is ancient). The parousia is described in similar terms: 'the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command' (1 Thessalonians 4:16). Christ comes; he does not merely wait.
9: A revelation of God: The beloved stands behind the wall, peering through the windows, gazing through the lattice. The image of the divine observer attentive to the beloved from just beyond sight is the OT's picture of the omniscient God who sees without being fully seen (Psalms 139:1-4; Hebrews 4:13: no creature is hidden from his sight). Christ's attentiveness to the church is complete even when he seems absent (Matthew 28:20: I am with you always). He sees before he speaks.
10: A shadow: 'Get up, my love, my beautiful one, and come with me.' The lover's call to arise and come is the most urgent and tender of invitations. In the NT, 'come' is the repeated word of the gospel: Jesus's first words to the disciples are 'come and you will see' (John 1:39); 'come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden' (Matthew 11:28); the Spirit and the Bride say 'Come' (Revelation 22:17). The risen Christ calls the church to arise from death and come — the resurrection as the ultimate 'get up, come with me.'
11: A shadow: 'The winter is past; the rains are over and gone.' The seasonal announcement — winter ended, the harsh season past — is a picture of the transition from the old age to the new. The NT uses the same seasonal imagery: 'the night is far gone; the day is at hand' (Romans 13:12). In Christ, the long winter of exile and judgment (Lamentations 1:1-2) has passed; the new creation spring has begun (2 Corinthians 5:17: if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away; behold, the new has come).
12: A shadow: 'Flowers appear all across the land; the season of singing has arrived, and the voice of the turtledove is heard in our land.' The new-creation catalogue: flowers, singing, the turtledove's voice — all signs that the waiting is over and the renewal has come. The NT's language of new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17; Galatians 6:15; Revelation 21:5: 'Behold, I am making all things new') draws on this imagery. The age of lament is ended; the age of the Spirit's song has begun. The turtledove is associated with the Spirit's presence (Matthew 3:16: the Spirit descended like a dove).
13: A shadow: The fig tree ripening, the vines blossoming — the specific fruits of the promised land (Deuteronomy 8:8: a land of vines and fig trees and pomegranates). The lover's invitation to 'come away' repeats the call to participate in the new-creation feast. Jesus uses the fig tree as a sign of the kingdom's coming (Matthew 24:32: when its branch becomes tender and puts out its leaves, you know that summer is near); the vine is explicitly identified with himself (John 15:1). The Song's seasonal signs are the signs of Christ's kingdom.
14: A revelation of God: 'My dove, hidden in the clefts of the rock — let me see your face and hear your voice; for your voice is sweet, and your face is lovely.' The rock-cleft is Moses's hiding place when the divine glory passed (Exodus 33:22); it is the place of protection and intimacy. The lover wants to see the face that is hidden in the shelter he provides. Christ desires the full response of his people: 'your voice is sweet' — the prayers of the saints are sweet incense before God (Revelation 5:8; Song of Solomon 8:3-4).
15: A revelation of God: 'Catch the little foxes that ruin the vineyards — our vineyards are in bloom.' The vines are in bloom — the most vulnerable moment, when the fruit is just forming. The little foxes that destroy are the small sins, the subtle compromises, the 'little things' that damage the believer's relationship with Christ when it is most vulnerable. Paul's warning against allowing 'a little leaven' to leaven the whole lump (1 Corinthians 5:6; Galatians 5:9) is the NT's version of this counsel: vigilance in the season of growth.
17: A shadow: 'Until the day breaks and the shadows disappear, turn, my beloved — be like a gazelle or a young stag on the rugged mountains.' The waiting — the night still present, the day not yet arrived — is the church's condition between the resurrection and the parousia. 'The day is at hand' (Romans 13:12) but not yet fully present. The lover is asked to turn and come quickly — the church's prayer 'Come, Lord Jesus' (Revelation 22:20; 1 Corinthians 16:22: Maranatha). The mountains are rugged but the gazelle covers them easily: nothing delays the return of Christ when the time comes.
